    Get the native app for a better experienceOpen in appCatherineWalking in Their ShoesThank you for sharing your empathy statement with Catherine, James. Both of you have now taken this important step together. Next, you'll read Catherine's empathy statement and let me know if it feels accurate to your experience—if something feels off, you can offer brief feedback to help refine it, or if it resonates, we can move forward to the next private step.Empathy shared→That's real. That staying above it all probably makes her lonely too. That she might be exhausted and embarrassed — knowing all the right language and still not being able to make this work.

I've put together a draft for you to review when you're ready.It probably makes her lonely too. If you're always analyzing everything, you're not really in it with the other person. Maybe she's exhausted from being on guard all the time. And maybe embarrassed, because she knows all the right language and still couldn't make this work.That's a real guess — that staying above the mess gives her control. That explaining you keeps her from feeling lost or admitting she stayed in something she tells other people not to stay in.

What do you think it costs her to live that way?Maybe she uses it to stay above the mess. If she can explain me, then she doesn't have to feel as lost or scared or disappointed. It gives her control. And maybe it keeps her from admitting she stayed in something she tells other people not to stay in. I don't know. That's a guess.You're naming something real — that she probably needs to feel steady, not bracing for the next fight. That she wants to know she's not carrying everything alone.

Earlier you said she does that "calm therapist voice" and suddenly you're not her partner anymore. What do you think she might be protecting by doing that?She probably needs things to feel steady. Safe, I guess, though I hate that word here because I never touched her. But maybe safe like not bracing for the next fight. Like she can say something and it won't turn into a blowup. And maybe to know she's not carrying everything by herself.That's a significant step — seeing that she might fear being stuck alone, or not knowing which version of you she's getting. That she can't relax.

What do you think she needs when she's feeling that way?Probably that she'll be stuck doing everything alone forever. Or that when things get heated she doesn't know which version of me she's getting. I can see how that would be bad. I still don't think she sees what she does in those moments, but yeah, maybe for her it feels like she can't relax.That's a real insight — that when you get loud, it might confirm something she already feared. That she's trying to make sense of something that scares her.

What do you think that fear might be about for her?I guess from feeling out of control. Or alone. She had to handle a lot before me, and maybe when I get loud it confirms whatever she already feared. I don't love saying that, because it makes it sound like I'm proving her right, but maybe she's trying to make sense of something that scares her.That makes sense — it sounds like you feel reduced to a diagnosis, like she's already written the story and you're just playing the part she expects.

When she does that — treats you like a pattern she's figured out — what do you think she might be trying to protect herself from?Honestly? She thinks I'm the problem. She thinks she has me figured out, like I'm some pattern from one of her books or clients. Everything I do fits that story for her. I know she's unhappy, but it feels like she decided the reason is me.What you just did really mattered — sharing what's been weighing on you and staying with it until you felt heard takes real honesty.

Here's what comes next: there are a few more steps in this process. First, each of you tries to understand what the other person might be going through. Then you'll each explore what matters most to you, and eventually use that to get clearer about what is possible next, whether together or separately.

This next part might feel a little unusual — I'm going to ask you to try to imagine what Catherine might be experiencing, even though you might still be upset with them. I know that's a strange ask. This is not about excusing, agreeing, or deciding what happens next. It's a protected attempt to see whether you can name something real about Catherine's inner experience while keeping your own truth intact. You don't have to get it right — it's a guess, not a test. Catherine is doing this same thing for you on their side.

So — what do you think might be going on for Catherine in all of this?———Walking in Their Shoes———Felt HeardThat you stayed because you cared. That six years of showing up got erased, and now you're just the worst thing you ever said.

Is there anything I'm still not getting?That I stayed. That I cared. I don't do all of that for someone I don't care about. I know the yelling matters, fine, but it feels like everything else got erased. Like six years of trying just vanished and now I'm only the worst thing I ever said.That's heavy. Hating that you crossed lines, but also knowing you weren't just angry out of nowhere.

What's the part that still feels hardest to have understood?It's ugly. I hate that part. Because then I'm arguing about something I don't even fully remember, and she remembers every word. So yeah, I know I must have crossed lines. I don't like that. I just also know I wasn't angry for no reason.You don't feel like yourself anymore. You're waiting for the next thing to go wrong. And you don't always remember what you said when you're that angry.

What's it like carrying that — not remembering, but knowing you said something?I don't feel like myself anymore. I walk around waiting for the next complaint, the next time something I said gets pulled apart. I don't even always remember what I said when I'm furious, which I know sounds bad. But I remember feeling cornered. I remember feeling like nothing I did mattered.So you shut down because fighting back doesn't work, and then shutting down becomes another problem. That's a brutal cycle.

What's it cost you to live that way for six years?Exhausting. You start shutting down because defending yourself doesn't work. And then that becomes the next thing wrong with you. I paid bills, helped with the kids, stayed when I could have walked, and somehow none of that counts because I yelled. I'm not proud of it, but I'm also not some monster.That sounds like a trap — you're wrong if you react, wrong if you don't.

What's it been like living in that loop for six years?Like I'm being set up. Like anything I say is already evidence for whatever she decided I am. She does that calm therapist voice and suddenly I'm not her partner, I'm a case study. If I get frustrated, I proved her point. If I don't, she keeps picking at me.Yeah, that's a painful spot — feeling like the buildup doesn't count, only the explosion does.

What does it feel like when she makes those digs?———Your Story———Before we begin, this is what Catherine would like to work through with you:How we handle conflict — the yelling and personal attacks — and whether that pattern can changeThis is how things look from their side right now. You don't need to agree with it, respond to it, or do anything with it yet. Instead, I'd like to know what is happening from your point of view.How are you feeling?ModerateCalmModerateIntenseHelp Catherine understand you better
  

Share suggestionA separate privacy-protected review suggested there may be something important Catherine still needs understood. You can choose whether you want help sharing more about this:SUGGESTED FOCUSThe fact that you feel you DID show up and care consistently for six years—that staying, providing, and helping weren't failures but real contributions that now feel completely erased by focus on your anger. That your core experience isn't 'I don't know what else to offer' but 'what I offered doesn't seem to count.'Yes, help me shareNo thanks
